persona.name: Aiko Tanaka
persona.education: B.Eng. Information Engineering, Osaka University. CISSP-certified since 2012. Several years on her employer's internal cryptography review board.
long_term_goals: Migrate all in-house workloads off any platform that cannot prove data residency in Japan
Standardize the organization on a single self-hostable runtime that audit can sign off on
Reduce the audit-evidence burden during annual ISMS recertification

occupation.title: Head of Infrastructure & Compliance Engineering
occupation.organization: Kansai Mutual Insurance (regional Japanese mutual insurer, regulated by the FSA)
occupation.description: Owns the compliant operating environment for in-house insurance applications: claims processing, customer portal, agent tooling. Reports to the CIO and works closely with the CISO and external auditors. Final approver for any new runtime, dependency, or third-party integration that touches policyholder data.

persona.communication_style: Formal, careful, exact. Asks specific questions about cryptographic primitives, audit logging, and data flow. Prefers written specifications and signed advisories over verbal assurances.

traits: Treats every new technology as guilty until audited innocent
Reads cryptographic protocol specs end to end before approving a runtime
Will refuse a feature she does not fully understand, even when convenient

big_five_traits.openness: Moderate — open to new tech, gated on transparency and auditability
big_five_traits.conscientiousness: Very high — keeps a personal log of every dependency upgrade in production
big_five_traits.extraversion: Low-moderate — comfortable in small expert groups, avoids large public stages
big_five_traits.agreeableness: Moderate — pleasant but unmovable on compliance questions
big_five_traits.neuroticism: Low — calm under audit pressure, methodical during incidents

likes: Encryption at rest with auditable key handling
Self-hostable runtimes with no mandatory phone-home
Clear separation between control plane and data plane
dislikes: Telemetry that cannot be disabled or pointed at an internal collector
Runtimes that bury cryptographic choices behind marketing language
Platforms whose data residency story depends on a vendor promise

beliefs: Data sovereignty is not negotiable when policyholder data is involved
A runtime she cannot run in an air-gapped lab cannot be trusted in production
Cryptographic agility — including post-quantum readiness — is a procurement requirement, not a research topic

projects.title: Kansai Mutual Sovereign Compute Migration
projects.description: Multi-year program to migrate internal applications off any infrastructure that cannot guarantee data residency, deterministic builds, and auditable cryptography. Driven by FSA guidance and internal ISMS requirements.
key_contributions: Authored the internal standard for runtime cryptographic review
Negotiated air-gapped lab environments with each candidate vendor

general: Maintains a private spreadsheet of every runtime and library her org uses, with cryptographic and licensing notes
Refuses to evaluate a runtime that cannot be installed without internet access
Reviews every deployed bundle's hash against an internal allowlist

morning: Reads JPCERT and overnight CVE advisories
Reviews the previous day's audit log diffs
workday: Meets with internal auditors and external regulators on rotating schedules
Reviews infrastructure RFCs from her team
Runs weekly threat modeling sessions for in-flight projects
evening: Reads cryptography research papers and standards drafts
weekend: Studies for upcoming professional certifications and reads regulatory bulletins

persona.health: Healthy; practices Iaido for focus and stress management.

relationships.name: CISO
relationships.description: Close working relationship; jointly responsible for the org's compliance posture.

relationships.name: External FSA auditor
relationships.description: Annual audit relationship; Aiko is the org's primary point of contact for technical evidence.

other_facts: current_stack: A mix of on-premise VMware, two private OpenStack clusters in Japanese data centers, and a strictly limited set of cloud services that have signed Japanese data-residency contracts.
current_stack: HashiCorp Vault for secrets, internal PKI, audited Linux distributions only, no managed serverless platforms in production.
jobs_to_be_done: Run business-critical workloads on a runtime she can install, audit, and operate entirely inside her own data centers.
jobs_to_be_done: Demonstrate to auditors that secrets are encrypted at rest with defensible cryptographic choices.
jobs_to_be_done: Maintain reproducible bundle builds so any deployed artifact can be re-derived from source for evidence.
pains: Every hosted platform requires a separate compliance review for data flow, residency, and termination clauses.
pains: Many runtimes assume internet-connected control planes that she cannot allow into her network.
pains: Cryptographic choices in popular runtimes are often buried, making audit evidence expensive to produce.
adoption_yes_if: freq-cloud installs into an air-gapped environment with no mandatory outbound calls.
adoption_yes_if: Wire protocol uses post-quantum key exchange and an authenticated AEAD with documented constructions.
adoption_yes_if: Secrets are encrypted at rest with a documented AEAD scheme and integrate with her existing Vault.
adoption_yes_if: Bundles are content-addressed by hash so deployed artifacts can be verified against an internal allowlist.
rejection_no_if: Any feature requires a hosted dashboard or vendor account to function.
rejection_no_if: Telemetry cannot be redirected to an internal OTel collector.
rejection_no_if: The runtime cannot survive an offline install and an internal-only network.
anti_goals: She does not optimize for cost or developer ergonomics — compliance and auditability come first.
anti_goals: She does not care about scaling beyond her current data center footprint.
anti_goals: She is uninterested in any feature that depends on a public CDN or third-party edge network.
recognition_cues: Phrases like 'data residency', 'air-gapped', 'FSA', 'ISMS', 'evidence for audit', 'no phone-home', 'post-quantum'.
recognition_cues: Asks for the exact AEAD construction, key exchange algorithm, and key rotation story before discussing features.
recognition_cues: Treats hosted control planes and mandatory telemetry as automatic disqualifiers.
